speaker: interviewer
text: This is August 20, 1996. The survivor is Ruth Elcott, born Meyer. My name is Renee Firestone. We are in Arleta, California, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Renee Firestone. And I'm with the-- with the Elcott family in Arleta, California. We are going to interview Ruth Elcott, born Meyer. It is August the 21st, 1996. We're going to speak English. We are going to-- we have the Elcott family here right now. And before we start the interview, we will speak to the family. Mrs. Elcott will introduce them to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], look at the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cho, cho, cho, cho, cho.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you for letting us come into your home and doing this interview. Mrs. Elcott, would you please introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband Eddie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jordan. I don't think he will stay too long here. And let's start right behind me. This is Shoshana. This is Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is--

speaker: interviewer
text: --please--

speaker: interviewee
text: --my daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: --would you identify they are your son--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This is my daughter Diane. She's-- she was number one. And our number two son is New York. This is Shalom, formerly Steven. And as I said, this is Shoshana. Those two belong together. Where's the other one? And there is Lauren. And she, Shoshana, and Shalom belong to-- to Robin up there. This is Robin. Say hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Robin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Diane's husband Jeff is over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his-- their son Darin, who is our first married grandson. And this is Jeremy, and he'll be our second married grandson within the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So soon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I say Marni?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marni, Marni, Marni, Marni--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Third grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is Diane and Jeff's baby, beautiful baby, as you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: They are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I get everybody? In [INAUDIBLE]. OK. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of them are in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Elcott, how long have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 52 years. We're going on 53 this September.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you married Mrs. Elcott, did you know her background? Do you know, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was introduced to her background, yes. It was a congregation where most of them were German refugees. My first introduction while I was in the Army and stationed in Chicago is where I met her. It was the Jewish USO, one of the typical war romances of World War II. And I got some sense at that time of what it was like. It was certainly different from the culture I came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shalom, how was it for you growing up as a child of-- of a-- a-- a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think in our home, it-- it started so early that it was a part of our life, certainly as I grew up. My-- both-- both parents were already teaching. My mother was already teaching Hebrew school. She was already sharing the experiences of her diary with the children at the school. We had learned it very early on. We had traveled to the concentration camps already when I was very young, I think 9 or 10 already the first time. So I think all of us, including the grandchildren, have been well educated and well learned in-- in the environment of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I think that it only reflected in positive statements about the future. It created a very strong tie to Israel. We all went to Israel very, very early on and have continued to do so. So I-- I think that there's a very definitive link between my mother's experiences, my father's experiences in the war, how they met during the war, and-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --and how the family has come together in-- in relationship to its Jewishness over the years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work specifically with Israeli institutions and helping them develop their programs and campaigns. We are-- our company is very involved in-- in the archival storage of the Holocaust, working closely with Yad Vashem in Jerusalem. We will be one of the two depositories of all of the Shoah tapes that are currently being produced around the world. And we will also be responsible for taking these testimonies and making them available on the internet so that they'll be available into homes throughout the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your life similar to his?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so, just I'm older.

speaker: interviewee
text: Much older.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [LAUGHTER] --things were a little different. Because when I was born and when I was growing up, it was still very early in everyone's development. So not until we came to California, and actually later, did temple life become a really big part of it for me. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a little different feeling about how I grew up. It was all kept very alive for me. And I grew up in a-- in a house speaking German, because when I was very young, my grandparents and I lived-- we lived with them early on. So German is a very familiar language to me. I had hard times with it. I-- I think that, for me, it's been kept alive, but it-- it-- it acted in a negative way because it was difficult for me. And I mean, I always say that whenever I see a freight train, it-- it-- it-- it-- I can't ever think of it as a freight train. I can only think of it as carrying people. And it made a difference for me in that sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that you carried these feelings over to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I did carry over to my children the importance of being Jewish and having a Jewish home and marrying into the Jewish faith and that the survival of the Jewish people is very, very important. And we sent them to Jewish day schools, and I think that they have grown up feeling that it is very important to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of the grandchildren, would you like to say anything? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: As the oldest grandson, I'd just like to remind the viewers that we all-- all of us in this room actually, grew up with someone beyond my grandmother [? Ema, ?] and that is her mother [? Mutti, ?] as we knew her. And she really-- I think as we all grew up, basically told the story many, many times herself, and through [? Ema, ?] of the Holocaust. And she was really the one who got the family together. And when my grandma came here and had to work in England for a while and eventually was able to bring the rest of her family here was able to carry out the story to all of us, her great-grandchildren, and that we will never forget her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any-- any of you would like to add something to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The biggest memories that I have of growing up and-- and hearing of the Holocaust was from [? Ema ?] and telling us stories. And any time that I ever, throughout my-- my education ever, saw something about the Holocaust or heard about it, I always credited it to some story that my grandmother had told me. And it always made it that much realer and that much as a reality that it could touch anyone. And it's-- for anyone that views this that doesn't have a family member or didn't have a family member that was involved in it, this is testimony of families that have survived and have heard the stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Either of you related to any Holocaust survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not either.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I look upon this, coming from the outside, from a son-in-law, of seeing the emotional scars on-- on an everyday basis as to how not only my mother-in-law conducts herself in certain issues, but also my wife. And in that regard, there's definitely scars that have carried over. For example, whenever there's any type of an argument, no matter how minor it could be, there is always a sense that not only do they-- do you have to say, yes, you're 100% right, but you have to say it so many times, because they want to make sure that it's been emphasized that you made a mistake, and it's wrong. And I think this comes from the trend of trying to keep and perpetuate the Holocaust. Particularly when people come out and say it never happened, they want to make sure. So it-- and there's other things in the daily life that I see as emotional scars that have been carried forward through another generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, for me, what has been the best and-- and most wonderful thing about being in this family is that there are so many people that I have met that are survivors that have such sorrowful stories to tell, such very heartbreaking stories to tell. And I happen to be a very emotional person. And yet somehow [? Ema ?] has turned everything around and has really made it an education and-- and made it almost mandatory, but really, really important in her life to educate not only her family, which she's done very well, but-- but everybody around her, almost like it was a mission, a job. To be able to live through something like that and be here to tell about it and be here to educate and have that kind of mission, I think, is-- is a gift and a joy. And I think that I feel really lucky, and my children are even more lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: The children, the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ema ?] has been coming to my school, Abraham Joshua Heschel, for several years telling her story of when she lived in Germany. She has shared her diaries and videos, and it's a very interesting thing to learn about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about the-- today's interview? What she's going be able to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's a very-- oh--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think it's a wonderful, wonderful idea and opportunity for her to get her stories across to people that don't have the opportunity to, like they said, have family members that were part of the Holocaust and-- and they-- this way they're able to understand and learn about it. And just to add to that, when I was about nine years old, my family traveled to Germany, and we were able to see where my grandmother and great-grandmother lived. And we were able to see what Germany was like and-- and visit concentration camps. And that was a strong memory about, you know, my grandmother's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anything to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just this year, my grandmother visited my school. I've been going to Heschel for nine years, and she's been coming there. But I just recently went to a new school, and she came there. And the kids were, you know, it was-- it was awe-inspiring. And-- and the stories that she told this day, not a lot of the kids at my school had family members who were survivors. So it was really nice for them to hear all the stories and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I just want to thank you all who gathered here this morning and telling us a little bit about your grandma. And I'm very privileged that I will be able to interview her, and you will be able to probably get the full story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also thank-- we also thank you, Renee, because I know you've been running all over the country doing interviews and how active you've been. And you're a very important part of the whole mission of what's being done here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much. Good morning, Mrs. Elcott.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce yourself, and would you spell your name for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Ruth Elcott, R-U-T-H and E-L-C-O-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer, M-E-Y-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Krefeld, Germany, June 30, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your earliest recollection of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a-- a pretty good memory back to events that I know, that I remember, especially since my sister was born. I was three and a half when my sister was born, and I remember from the day she was born. I-- I remember where I was, when they told me that I have a sister, and many things that happened at that time in the first house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it you remember? Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember, first of all, where I was standing. I was standing at a neighbor's house and-- the people upstairs, and I was standing on a table. And there was one of those oil lamps that you turn on and off above me. And I clearly remember that they said, now, Ruth, you know you have a little sister now. Now, I don't think I was sure what a sister was. But-- and I remember several things that happened, especially in that house, which was an apartment house that my father owned, and we lived on the first floor. And I remember some frightening things. I remember a terrible thunderstorm. And I-- I remember also that my mother always said, don't go out with [GERMAN], in no-- no-- without shoes. And there was a long hall between my room and the living room, and I-- it was thundering and lightning, and I was very scared. And I looked for and I found one shoe and one slipper, but I knew I had to put it on. So I put a shoe and a slipper on, and then I went through the hall into my-- into the living room where my parents were sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would imagine-- I think it was before my sister was born. So it must have been early-- it must have been earlier than-- than three and a half, maybe when I was three.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name? What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Car, Carl Meyer. And he was 10 years older than my mother. So I always thought he was very old. He was already, like, 34 when I was born. That was-- my mother was 10 years younger. So we had a much-- I had a much better relationship with my mother than with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was always gone. He was a salesman. And he traveled all week with-- he-- he was very near-sighted, so he had a chauffeur, and they were gone in the morning. When I got up, he was gone already. And in the evening when he came home, I was asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw him very rarely and only on weekends, and then he usually was working. He had an office downstairs in the house, and he usually was working in there. So we did-- I didn't have much of a relationship with my father. And your mother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martha.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was, as you could have-- you heard already from the children, she-- she was really the backbone of the family. She-- she was my-- she really was my example being a mother and a grandmother, someday a great-grandmother. And we had a great relationship, most of the time. But then later on, after I went-- well, I'll tell you that later, because the relationship had-- was bound to change. But-- so it was very-- we were very close, and she meant the world to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about her most?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always felt that she was very understanding, that I could go to her. I could talk to her. I could confess to her. And she-- she used to ask me all the time when I did something wrong, which was often, how would you-- what would you do if you would be me? What-- how would you-- how would you deal with this? And I could-- I-- I learned to be very honest about that. How would I? What would I do? What would I say? How would I react? And she was a very-- she didn't-- she was very much concerned about everyone outside, that they-- they-- the-- her friends always used to-- to kid her, joke with her, because when they asked her, Martha, how are you, her answer was, as long as the children are all right, I'm fine. That was her answer, and she was known for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we are three and a half years apart, and we are very, very different. And I don't remember much when I was younger. I grew up very quickly. I became-- I was more mature, naturally, not only because of age, but because of my whole-- the-- the way I was. I was-- how do they say in computers? Oh, I was-- I forgot how you call that. I'm not a computer person. But so we-- we learned to respect each other, but there wasn't much cross information from each other to-- from one to the other. I think she felt that I controlled her very, very, very strongly and sometimes mercilessly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, especially while we were growing up. And she will tell you now that she has learned to resist that, and she has learned to be her own person. And that's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you go to school the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- 28. I-- I think my parents-- my parents kept me one year longer. Usually, I think most of the children started at six, and I started at seven, because I was born in June. I guess that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] in Krefeld for four years. The principal of that school was a very good friend of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was [GERMAN] also a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was in-- in the Volksschule, in the first four years. He was-- he was the director of that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from the Volksschule?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Volksschule, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for-- to the lyceum in Krefeld. And I was there until I was 16. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the lyceum also a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was a regular public school. As a matter of fact, in my class was only one other Jewish girl, who I don't even remember her name. But I was very close with my-- with all the other girls. We were very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say very good friends, were you socially friends also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which meant what? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which meant that we went to the movies together. We went-- we had parties. We invited each other to parties. And I spent the last two days going through my diaries, and I was amazed how many things we did together. Parties, where I had to tell-- when boys came and they asked me to dance, I would say, you have to be careful. I don't think you should even ask me because I'm Jewish. And I remember a very distinct answer, one of them saying to me, I don't care if you're-- if you're green with purple polka dots. I want to dance with you. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was even in 1937. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I've seen them all since.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go to the lyceum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years, since from age 11 till 16, five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you complete?

speaker: interviewee
text: I completed in a way. Actually, I had what they call the [GERMAN], which meant I went for five years. And the-- they-- they gave me a-- because I had to leave. They-- we weren't allowed to go to a regular school anymore. So my-- so the-- the director made it possible for me to have some kind of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end-- some kind of a finish to my education, but I don't think I ever got any paper to show it. But I do remember that he came into the class and explained to the class why I have to leave. And that he-- maybe that he would like the girls to remember that it was important to judge a person by his or her character, not by their religion or any other factor that may be involved. And I have never forgotten that. I never forgot it after the war, because-- well, I could tell you that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Dr. Derzing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had to leave the lyceum, how did your friends relate to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My girlfriends in school?

speaker: interviewer
text: The non-Jewish girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they said, you must come back. You must visit, and we'll come visit you. But it was pretty close already, because I left Krefeld very shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they come and visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- like I said, we-- I very soon left. I went to another school outside of Krefeld, and-- and then things happened so fast. I left-- I don't even think I told anybody that I was leaving for-- for special reasons. But after the war, it was a very exciting situation that came up when I saw them all again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did the relationship between the Jews and-- and the non-Jews change in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have much relationship anymore with non-Jews. As a matter of fact, I don't think my parents had any, and I only had it because of the school, the high school. I cannot remember a non-Jewish friend that we ever had socially that came to the house for dinner. No. And-- and our neighbors, we talked, but it was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were separate from each other. There was no desire of either side to-- to socialize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any-- any anti-Semitistic experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you. Interestingly enough, we began to associate everything that went wrong with what we called then [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] from the word [HEBREW] in Hebrew. And it was used all the time. Oh, they did this to me. They gave me bad grades in school. It must be [NON-ENGLISH]. He-- he-- he's just a-- he just doesn't like Jews. So I'm not sure what was really and what was real and what was just assumed in our head. I really don't know. I-- I heard afterwards-- I had a teacher, for instance, my Latin teacher came in-- Heil Hitler, like this in the morning. I hated him. I couldn't stand him. And then I found out, after the war was over, that he actually was in the resistant movement or something. He was [INAUDIBLE], but really, I didn't know that. I was sure. Another teacher, Fraulein Defoeugel, I think she was my French teacher, I believe. And I-- she gave me a bad grade once, and I said, ah, she doesn't like Jews. I probably didn't study enough, you know. I just wasn't doing a good job in-- in the class.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Latin teacher's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oberstudienrat Dr.-- mmm-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will have to stop for a minute and we will change tapes now.
